tweets.text: @isuryatk Yeah.. there was a lot.. we did some good stuff then but lots of lessons learned for II

tweets.text:
Around this time the majority of compute at Stability AI was being used on building open LLMs but:

1. We didn’t do the easy thing of training GPT-J / Neo-X more (pre chinchilla!)

2. Couldn’t make it safe / tried to do too many changes to formula (￼Pile 2, Reddit crawls etc)

We succeeded in building sota/frontier models of other all types that worked on the edge; but LLMs really were difficult to get working in consumer hardware then & even harder to do “right”

In the end Mistral & Meta got the first wave of good quality open LLMs before the Chinese took over 

I honestly think OpenAI, Anthropic etc should have released really good quality edge open models with community interaction focused on how best to align these

Lots of lessons to learn & Sam is right that it would almost have been safer given the better underlying datasets they had by then plus early RL.

Also that it would have dried up some of the funding environment but..
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Sam Altman emails OpenAI board

October 1, 2022 https://t.co/BcPuCedGz4
tweets.full_text:
Around this time the majority of compute at Stability AI was being used on building open LLMs but:

1. We didn’t do the easy thing of training GPT-J / Neo-X more (pre chinchilla!)

2. Couldn’t make it safe / tried to do too many changes to formula (￼Pile 2, Reddit crawls etc)

We succeeded in building sota/frontier models of other all types that worked on the edge; but LLMs really were difficult to get working in consumer hardware then & even harder to do “right”

In the end Mistral & Meta got the first wave of good quality open LLMs before the Chinese took over 

I honestly think OpenAI, Anthropic etc should have released really good quality edge open models with community interaction focused on how best to align these

Lots of lessons to learn & Sam is right that it would almost have been safer given the better underlying datasets they had by then plus early RL.

Also that it would have dried up some of the funding environment but..

The stats beyond the final score:
If you are wondering whether Argentina surpassed Spain in any of the underlying statistics, it wasn't in shots on goal, possession, corners, passes, or anything positive. It was in the fouls they committed and the yellow cards they received. And of course the red card, but then that keeps their lead on this stat for all world cups!
#worldcup #argentina #ArgentinaVsEspaña #spain @FIFAWorldCup

The third-worlding of America.

Legitimate businesses and restaurants have to spend money on compliance, health inspections, permits, taxes, insurance, legal wages, etc.

Meanwhile, illegal immigrants set up shop anywhere they want. No inspections. No regulations. Cash only. Far-left officials look the other way and allow them to undercut Americans while normalizing third-world decline in our cities.

tweets.text:
Xi Jinping is not an OSS warrior. And yet he's now calling (as of 2 days ago) for open weight releases from Chinese labs.

Why?

Is he just altruistic? Is it a new Belt and Road initiative? Or does he want to compress private investment in the US Labs and push China to build a lead?

It's worth thinking about these questions seriously. I don't know the answer, but if you're not even thinking about the geopolitical questions here, you're missing the most important technological battle of the decade playing out right before your eyes.

Open source AI is great. It's got enormous positive externalities and as an investor and user of this stuff, it benefits me tremendously. I want it to continue, and I want the labs duopoly get eroded.

But there's a bigger game being played here. To China, OSS is not an ideology but a piece on that chess board.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
@hosseeb @deanwball Soooo, releasing Linux was dumping? 
Apache, MySQL, PHP? 
HTTP, TCP/IP, OpenSSL, OpenSSH? 
Libjpeg, VLC?
The open source software stack of the mobile communication network?
Signal?
PyTorch?
Llama?

tweets.text:
The balance sheet considerations dominate here. Company holds ~40% of the token supply on the balance sheet, and management overwhelmingly hold their token interests through the balance sheet exposure.

Stockholders selling their tokens happens all the time with devcos, but doesn't result in a bevy of lawsuits trying to get devcos to pivot.
tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text:
It's not dumping because you can dump back? 

If China forces US labs to cut R&D budgets because private markets get spooked when token prices compress, and then assume China nationalizes their frontier labs straight away and we don't, why wouldn't that mean they can develop a meaningful lead? The politics of doing the same in the US is awful given the public perception of AI.

At this point it's not just a corporate strategy, it's a geopolitical/economic one.

https://t.co/u9TbCv95Jt

tweets.text:
Thoughts from that final:

Argentina absolutely pathetic, both in behaviour and in performance.

Messi a little snitch rat.

Couldn't have been happier to see Enzo sent off.

Disallowed goal was a joke (same happened to Spence in the semi)

To not have a shot on target in a Final is absolutely awful.

Tried the same old tactics, batter the opponents, try to hurt them, wind them up, foul to break up any momentum, Spain rose above it all.

Complete domination of the game from back to front from Spain, taught them a lesson.

As soon as the game switched, they started throwing themselves to the floor.

The worst losers I've ever seen in a final, a full taste of their own medicine. The aftermath is shameful and shows you exactly the mentality of that team.

Embarrassing for them, you could have played all night and you weren't scoring a goal. Football won, and rightly so.

Rat cunts, fuck off back home with fuck all to show for it.

thread_tweets.text:
"Bad actors" is not an argument, it's just an ad hominem fallacy. Attacking people is a waste of time; instead one should criticize ideas.

Knots uptake is partially a reactionary movement by folks who don't understand the nuances of the protocol and partially a sybil attack.

The alternative is that the status quo is not harming Bitcoin; the network is operating just fine. The biggest problem is that not enough people are actually using Bitcoin.

tweets.text:
Interesting point. The first one feels temporary.
The second one more durable. But hash power or energy going to Bitcoin mining doesn't determine its price (the network difficulty adjusts if miners go offline to keep the same pace of block mining). Long term, Bitcoin price is mostly a measure of how much people fear inflation, and there seems to no end in sight to democracies everywhere running deficits.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
after squatting and deadlifting for a minute

it made me realize that most people in their daily non-lifting lives have no idea how to sit down, bend over to pick something up off of the floor, or squat down on the floor

in a way which minimizes the risk of injury

no one ever taught them how to do it

the result is deteriorating mobility as you get older

tweets.text:
i'm in favor of rapid data center build out in the US

because most people don't realize it's, but it may be our last hope

we have no manufacturing left. and you can't wave a magic wand and bring it back. it will take much more deliberate and coherent effort then we're expending now, and it will take many decades. we're talking entire supply chains and speed to iterate through long-established colocation between suppliers, producers, and customers in Shenzen, etc.

most of our services economy, which is what replaced the old manufacturing economy, will get cannibalized by AI, and far more quickly than a lot of people expect

our financial sector depends on the primacy of the USD which is no longer a foregone conclusion

so what's the point of data centers then?

China can release open weight models, but they can't simply clone compute. they have to produce it, and right now they don't have access to all of the latest compute tech. but they are very, very resourceful and will quickly find ways to do more with less, so any current US advantage will not be insurmountable

but IF the US gets to self-improving AI which can generate novel research AND has the most compute AND gets a head start on robotics AND can get those robotics supply chains created domestically (some big ifs tbh), then the US still has a shot

if it doesn't do these things, then the US has possibly already lost. even worse, we're bleeding most of our soft power and global goodwill at the moment

i'm not being hyperbolic, but this may be our last stand. the vast majority of people just don't understand it yet

if business leaders and policy makers talked about it like this, and provided a vision of how it would benefit Americans, they'd likely find a lot more support around AI (study American support for various policies during the Cold War)
